course_info.title: Shakespeare Studies
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 3 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Shakespeare in Context: The Elizabethan Theater and Its Conditions
learning_objectives: Understand the material conditions of Elizabethan performance: the Globe, the company system, and the audience
Analyze how theatrical conventions — no sets, boy actors, outdoor daylight — shaped Shakespeare's dramatic technique
Distinguish between the quarto and folio texts and their implications for editorial choices
readings: Greenblatt, Will in the World, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Shakespeare wrote for a specific theater, a specific company, and a specific audience. Understanding those material conditions is essential to reading his plays as scripts designed for performance, not as literature designed for the study.
key_concepts: The Globe Theatre: thrust stage, three levels, standing groundlings, no artificial lighting
The company system: Shakespeare as actor-shareholder-playwright, writing for known actors
Boy actors playing women: how the convention shapes Shakespeare's gender comedy
Quarto vs. Folio: the textual problems of plays not published by their author
The soliloquy convention: direct address to the audience as a theatrical contract
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing the quarto and folio texts of Hamlet's 'To be or not to be' soliloquy, analyzing how textual variants affect interpretation

review.value: The Globe's stage extended into the audience on three sides, creating an intimate relationship between actor and spectator that Shakespeare exploited through soliloquies, asides, and direct address

Many Shakespeare plays exist in multiple early printed versions that differ significantly; editors must choose between them, and every edition of Shakespeare is an interpretation, not a transparent window onto the original

class_schedule.topic: Comedy: A Midsummer Night's Dream and the Transformative Power of Theater
learning_objectives: Analyze Shakespeare's comic structure: the journey from order through chaos to a new, transformed order
Identify the play-within-a-play as a metatheatrical device exploring theater's relationship to reality
Understand how the four plot strands (court, lovers, fairies, mechanicals) mirror and comment on each other

lecture_notes.introduction: A Midsummer Night's Dream is Shakespeare's most sustained meditation on the theater itself. The four interwoven plots all explore the same question: what is the relationship between imagination, desire, and reality?
key_concepts: Comic structure: the green world pattern — departure from court, transformation in the forest, return to a new order
The four plot strands and their parallels: each group (courtiers, lovers, fairies, mechanicals) enacts a version of desire and illusion
Bottom's transformation: the comic version of metamorphosis as a metaphor for what theater does to us
The mechanicals' play: bad theater that paradoxically demonstrates what good theater requires
Theseus vs. Hippolyta on imagination: 'The lunatic, the lover, and the poet / Are of imagination all compact'
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of Theseus's speech on imagination and Hippolyta's response, analyzing how the play refuses to side with either rationalism or fancy
setup_requirements: Complete text of A Midsummer Night's Dream
Performance clips for comparison (film and stage)

review.value: Northrop Frye's term for the space outside the social order — forest, island, countryside — where comic transformation occurs before the characters return to society renewed
Theater that is aware of itself as theater: the mechanicals' play-within-a-play forces the audience to reflect on the conventions they themselves are accepting in watching Shakespeare's play

class_schedule.topic: Tragedy: Hamlet and the Problem of Action
learning_objectives: Analyze the critical debate over Hamlet's delay and its multiple interpretive frameworks
Identify how the play uses metatheatrical devices — the play-within-a-play, the antic disposition — to explore the relationship between performance and identity
Understand the soliloquy as a dramatic form that gives the audience privileged access to consciousness
readings: Hamlet (complete, Acts I-III for this session)

lecture_notes.introduction: Hamlet is the most written-about play in the English language, and it has generated more interpretive controversy than any other literary work. At its center is a question that has haunted readers for four centuries: why does Hamlet delay?
key_concepts: The delay: psychological, philosophical, and theatrical interpretations
The play-within-the-play: 'The Mousetrap' as Hamlet's attempt to use theater to reveal truth
The antic disposition: is Hamlet performing madness, or has the performance become real?
Soliloquy as consciousness: 'To be or not to be' as the audience's direct access to Hamlet's mind
The revenge tragedy genre: Shakespeare's transformation of a crude genre into philosophical drama
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of 'To be or not to be,' analyzing it not as a speech about suicide but as a meditation on the relationship between thought and action — the very problem the play dramatizes
setup_requirements: Complete text of Hamlet (Norton edition recommended)

review.value: The central interpretive problem: why does Hamlet not immediately avenge his father? Answers range from psychological (Oedipal guilt), to philosophical (paralysis by thought), to theatrical (the play needs five acts)
Hamlet's decision to 'put an antic disposition on' — to feign madness — raises the question of whether the performance eventually becomes real, blurring the line between role-playing and identity

class_schedule.topic: History: Henry V and the Politics of Performance
learning_objectives: Analyze Henry V as both a patriotic celebration and a critique of political power and military violence
Identify how the Chorus's apologies for theatrical limitation become a commentary on the relationship between rhetoric and reality
Understand the play's ambiguity as a feature, not a failure, of Shakespeare's political dramaturgy

lecture_notes.introduction: Is Henry V a patriotic hero or a ruthless politician? The play refuses to decide — and that refusal is its genius. Shakespeare gives us a king who is both inspiring and terrifying, and asks us to judge for ourselves.
key_concepts: The Chorus: apologies for theatrical limitation that simultaneously assert theater's power to create kings and battles from nothing
Henry's rhetoric: the Crispin's Day speech as a masterclass in political persuasion
The dark underside: the killing of prisoners, the threat to Harfleur, the execution of Bardolph
The two readings: Branagh's patriotic film vs. the antiwar reading — both fully supported by the text
Political ambiguity as Shakespeare's method: the plays dramatize political problems without resolving them
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing Henry's speech before Harfleur ('Once more unto the breach') alongside his private soliloquy on the burden of kingship, showing how Shakespeare gives us both the public performance and its private cost
setup_requirements: Film clips from Branagh and Olivier versions

review.value: A unique Shakespearean device: a speaker who stands outside the play to apologize for theater's inability to represent reality, while demonstrating that theater's power lies precisely in its demand for the audience's imaginative collaboration

Shakespeare's history plays present political leaders as simultaneously admirable and dangerous, allowing the audience to experience the seductive power of political rhetoric while recognizing its manipulation

class_schedule.topic: The Late Romances: The Tempest and the Art of Letting Go
learning_objectives: Analyze The Tempest as Shakespeare's meditation on artistic power, forgiveness, and the renunciation of control
Identify postcolonial readings of the play that reframe Prospero as colonizer and Caliban as colonized
Understand the play's epilogue as Shakespeare's farewell to the theater

lecture_notes.introduction: The Tempest is Shakespeare's last solo-authored play. It is about a magician who orchestrates a drama, controls every character's movements, and then renounces his power. It is impossible not to read it as Shakespeare's reflection on his own art.
key_concepts: Prospero as playwright: his magic is theatrical direction — he stages events, controls timing, and manipulates perception
The masque: Prospero's pageant for Ferdinand and Miranda as art within art within art
Caliban and colonialism: postcolonial readings that reframe the play as a drama of dispossession and cultural domination
'Our revels now are ended': Prospero's speech as Shakespeare's meditation on the transience of theater and life
The epilogue: Prospero asks the audience to set him free — the artist's final dependence on the audience
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of 'Our revels now are ended,' analyzing how the speech moves from theatrical metaphor to existential meditation: the actors are spirits, the stage is the world, and both will dissolve
setup_requirements: Postcolonial criticism excerpt (Cesaire or Fernandez Retamar)

review.value: Postcolonial readings argue that Prospero's control of the island, enslavement of Caliban, and imposition of his language and values constitute a colonial allegory — a reading supported by the play's setting and power dynamics

Prospero's final address — 'Now my charms are all o'erthrown' — breaks the dramatic illusion entirely, asking the audience's applause to 'set me free,' collapsing the distinction between character and actor, magic and theater

questions.question: How does the metatheatrical device of the mechanicals' play in A Midsummer Night's Dream comment on Shakespeare's own theatrical practice?
questions.answer: The mechanicals' terrible play demonstrates what happens when theater fails: they over-explain, refuse to trust the audience's imagination, and break illusion by reassuring the audience that the lion is not a real lion. By showing bad theater, Shakespeare implicitly argues for his own method — trusting the audience, maintaining illusion, and using language to create reality rather than explaining it away.

rubric.key: Identifies the mechanicals' play as metatheatre
Explains how their failures define good theater by contrast
Connects to Shakespeare's own theatrical principles

questions.question: What are the implications of the quarto/folio textual problem for reading Shakespeare? Why can we not simply read 'what Shakespeare wrote'?
questions.answer: Shakespeare did not publish his plays; the texts that survive come from multiple sources — quartos printed from actors' memories or the author's drafts, and the posthumous First Folio compiled by colleagues. Many plays exist in versions that differ significantly, and editors must choose between variants. Every edition is therefore an editorial interpretation, not a direct transmission of Shakespeare's words.

rubric.key: Notes that Shakespeare did not oversee publication
Identifies every edition as an editorial interpretation

quizzes.topic: Tragedy, History, and the Late Plays

questions.question: Describe two different critical interpretations of Hamlet's delay. Which interpretation do you find most persuasive, and why?
questions.answer: The psychological interpretation (Coleridge, Bradley) argues Hamlet thinks too much to act. The Oedipal interpretation (Freud, Jones) argues Hamlet cannot kill Claudius because Claudius has fulfilled Hamlet's own unconscious wish. The theatrical interpretation argues delay is a structural necessity of the five-act revenge tragedy. The student should defend one interpretation with reference to textual evidence.

rubric.key: Selects one and provides a reasoned defense

questions.question: How can Henry V be read as both a patriotic celebration and a critique of political power? Cite specific scenes that support each reading.
questions.answer: The patriotic reading is supported by the Crispin's Day speech, Henry's common touch ('a little touch of Harry in the night'), and the English victory against overwhelming odds. The critical reading is supported by the threat to Harfleur (mass rape and infanticide), the order to kill prisoners, and the Archbishop's cynical justification for war. Shakespeare provides evidence for both readings without resolving the contradiction.

rubric.key: Provides specific evidence for the patriotic reading
Provides specific evidence for the critical reading
Recognizes that the play sustains both readings simultaneously

major_assignments.title: Shakespeare Performance Analysis
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,500-3,000 word essay comparing two performances (film, stage recording, or live performance) of a single Shakespeare play. The essay must analyze how directorial and acting choices interpret the text differently, using close reading of the script to ground the comparison.
objectives: Analyze the relationship between text and performance in Shakespeare
Compare directorial interpretations grounded in close reading of the script
Demonstrate understanding of how theatrical choices create meaning
Integrate at least two secondary sources on Shakespeare in performance
deliverables: 2,500-3,000 word comparative essay in MLA format
Works Cited page with play text, performance details, and scholarly sources
Scene log documenting key moments in both performances

criteria: Specific directorial and acting choices are identified and analyzed
Analysis connects performance choices to textual interpretation
Comparison reveals what each performance emphasizes and what it sacrifices
Performance analysis is grounded in close reading of the script
Close reading of the script is accurate and detailed
Knowledge of the play's critical tradition informs the analysis
Scholarly sources on Shakespeare in performance are integrated
Analysis demonstrates understanding of the text's interpretive possibilities
Prose is clear, engaging, and well-organized
MLA format is followed correctly
Performance descriptions are vivid and precise
Essay meets length requirements
Insightful comparison of two performances grounded in close reading
Detailed analysis of how directorial choices create textual interpretation
Effective integration of performance criticism scholarship
Clear, engaging academic prose

major_assignments.title: Research Essay on a Shakespeare Critical Debate
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,500-3,000 word research essay that enters a specific critical debate about a Shakespeare play. The essay must summarize at least three scholarly positions, evaluate their strengths and weaknesses, and articulate the student's own position with textual evidence.
objectives: Engage with a specific critical debate in Shakespeare studies
Summarize and evaluate multiple scholarly positions
Articulate an original position supported by close reading
Demonstrate advanced research and argumentation skills
deliverables: 2,500-3,000 word research essay in MLA format
Works Cited page with at least 5 scholarly sources

criteria: At least three scholarly positions are accurately represented
Positions are evaluated for strengths and weaknesses, not merely summarized
Student's own position emerges from engagement with the debate
Research demonstrates thorough familiarity with the critical conversation
Student's position is grounded in close reading of the play
Textual evidence is specific and well-analyzed
Analysis demonstrates sophisticated understanding of the play
Argument is original, not merely echoing one existing scholar
Prose is clear, precise, and appropriate for advanced literary scholarship
Essay is well-organized with effective transitions
MLA format is accurate throughout
Length requirements are met
Substantive engagement with a critical debate in Shakespeare studies
Original argument grounded in careful close reading
Effective evaluation of multiple scholarly positions
Polished academic prose demonstrating advanced research skills

performance_analysis.description: Comparative analysis of two performances of a Shakespeare play

research_essay.description: Research essay entering a critical debate in Shakespeare studies

quizzes.description: Quizzes on plays, historical context, and critical approaches

seminar_participation.description: Quality of contributions to seminar discussion

final_examination.description: Passage identification, close reading, and comparative essay on unseen passages

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction. Active seminar participation is required at the 400-level.
course_policies.late_submissions: Essays penalized 5% per day late. Extensions require 48 hours advance arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: All written work must be original individual work. Seminar discussion is collaborative.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism results in a zero and referral to the academic integrity board. All sources must be cited. AI-generated text is not permitted.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Norton Shakespeare or equivalent complete works, access to performance recordings, word processing software, library database access, and LMS access.
